source.title: Efficient production of d-lactate from methane in a lactate-tolerant strain of Methylomonas sp. DH-1 generated by adaptive laboratory evolution

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Methane is an abundant, low-cost carbon source, but metabolic engineering of methanotrophs for chemical production is challenging. Prior attempts to produce lactic acid (LA) from methane in methanotrophs achieved low titers (<1 g/L), partly due to LA toxicity. This study aimed to overcome LA toxicity in Methylomonas sp. DH-1 by generating an LA-tolerant strain via adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE), identifying the genetic basis of tolerance, and engineering the strain for improved D-lactate production from methane.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Summarizes the problem (LA toxicity limits production), approach (ALE to generate tolerant strain JHM80, genome sequencing to identify watR mutation, metabolic engineering with D-LDH and glgA deletion), and results (7.5-fold higher D-LA production, final titer 1.19 g/L, yield 0.245 g/g CH4).
process_signals: LA toxicity is a limiting factor for high-level LA production in methanotrophs.
ALE generated LA-tolerant strain JHM80.
2-bp deletion in watR promoter partly responsible for tolerance.
Overexpression of watR in wild-type increased LA tolerance.
Integration of D-LDH from L. mesenteroides and glgA deletion in JHM80 led to 7.5-fold higher D-LA production.
Medium optimization (pH control, extra nitrogen) further improved titer to 1.19 g/L.

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: Methane is a promising feedstock for bioconversion. Methanotrophs can use methane, but metabolic engineering is limited. Previous LA production in methanotrophs (M. buryatense, M. alcaliphilum) achieved <1 g/L, likely due to LA toxicity. Methylomonas sp. DH-1 lacks native LDH, advantageous for optically pure LA production.
process_signals: Prior LA titers in methanotrophs were low (0.5-0.8 g/L).
LA toxicity suspected as a major limiting factor.
Methylomonas sp. DH-1 has no native LDH, enabling pure D-LA production.
metrics: Previous max LA titer: 0.8 g/L in M. buryatense 5GB1S
failures_or_limits: Low LA tolerance in wild-type methanotrophs limits production.

section_gists.heading: Development of LA-tolerant strains by adaptive laboratory evolution
section_gists.gist: Wild-type Methylomonas sp. DH-1 growth severely inhibited by LA (tolerance up to 0.5 g/L at pH 6.8). ALE performed by serial transfer with increasing LA (0.5 to 8.0 g/L) over 35 subcultures. Evolved strains JHM30 (tolerant to 3.0 g/L) and JHM80 (tolerant to 8.0 g/L) isolated. Evolved strains showed comparable growth to wild-type without LA, but survived at inhibitory LA concentrations.
process_signals: Initial LA tolerance of wild-type: only up to 0.5 g/L.
ALE started at 0.5 g/L LA, increased to 8.0 g/L over 35 subcultures.
JHM30 survived at 3.0 g/L, JHM80 at 8.0 g/L.
No growth defect in evolved strains under normal conditions.
methods: Adaptive laboratory evolution: serial transfer in NMS medium with increasing LA (0.5 to 8 g/L), pH 6.8, 35 subcultures.

section_gists.heading: Identification of genes responsible for the enhanced LA tolerance
section_gists.gist: Whole genome sequencing of JHM30 and JHM80 revealed a 2-bp (TT) deletion in the intergenic region between AYM39_21115 and AYM39_21120 (watR). JHM80 also had a nonsense mutation in fliE (Gln49→stop). The TT deletion increased expression of watR and downstream genes smtM and rstM. Overexpression of watR alone in wild-type increased LA tolerance. Deletion or overexpression of smtM and rstM did not affect tolerance. WatR is a LysR-type transcription factor; its target genes mediating tolerance are unknown.
process_signals: TT deletion in promoter region of watR operon found in both JHM30 and JHM80.
TT deletion upregulates watR, smtM, rstM.
Overexpression of watR in wild-type increased LA tolerance, confirming its role.
smtM and rstM not directly involved in tolerance.
fliE nonsense mutation in JHM80 may contribute but not validated.
WatR likely regulates other genes; mechanism unknown.
failures_or_limits: Mechanism of TT deletion enhancing transcription not elucidated.
Role of fliE mutation not tested.

section_gists.heading: D-Lactate production in JHM80
section_gists.gist: Four heterologous D-LDH genes tested in JHM80 with glgA deletion. Lm.LDH (L. mesenteroides) and Ld.LDH (L. delbrueckii) gave highest LA (192 and 187 mg/L). JHM86 (JHM80 + Lm.LDH + ΔglgA) produced 750 mg/L D-LA with periodic methane feeding, vs. 100 mg/L in wild-type background (JHM14). Flask-scale with pH control and extra nitrogen (2× KNO3) increased titer to 1.19 g/L, yield 0.245 g/g CH4, productivity 0.008 g/L h. Growth stopped after 96 h possibly due to nutrient depletion; extra nitrogen extended growth to 120 h.
process_signals: LDH genes integrated into chromosome under native glgBA promoter.
Lm.LDH selected for further work.
JHM86 produced 7.5-fold more D-LA than wild-type equivalent, confirming tolerance importance.
pH control with NaOH increased production by 15%.
Nutrient limitation (nitrogen) suspected; doubling KNO3 extended growth and increased titer.
Methane consumption: 51% of supplied methane.
methods: Chromosomal integration of heterologous D-LDH genes
Periodic methane feeding (20% v/v every 24 h)
metrics: Initial D-LA titer with Lm.LDH: 192 mg/L
JHM86 titer: 750 mg/L (serum bottle), 1.19 g/L (flask, optimized)
Methane consumption: 60.8 mg at 144 h (51% of supplied)
failures_or_limits: Growth stopped after 96 h in standard medium, likely due to nitrogen depletion.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: LA toxicity is a major bottleneck in methanotroph LA production; previous max titer matched tolerance level. ALE and watR overexpression successfully increased tolerance. WatR is a LysR-type regulator; its target genes unknown. Methylomonas sp. DH-1 lacks native LDH, enabling optically pure D-LA. JHM86 produced 7.5-fold more than wild-type, proving tolerance is critical. Further improvements possible by optimizing fermentation and exploring watR regulon.
process_signals: Previous LA production limited by tolerance, not just pathway flux.
watR overexpression alone partially recapitulates tolerance phenotype.
WatR target genes remain to be identified.
Lack of native LDH is advantageous for pure D-LA.
Further gains possible by addressing nutrient limitations and understanding WatR regulon.
failures_or_limits: Mechanism of TT deletion effect unclear.
Productivity still low for industrial application.

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: ALE-generated JHM80 enabled effective D-LA production from methane. JHM86 produced 1.19 g/L D-LA, yield 0.245 g/g CH4, productivity 0.01 g/L h. watR overexpression identified as key tolerance mechanism.
process_signals: ALE is effective for improving LA tolerance.
watR is a key gene for LA tolerance.
Combining tolerance with metabolic engineering achieved highest reported D-LA titer from methane.

section_gists.heading: Laboratory adaptive evolution
section_gists.gist: ALE protocol: Methylomonas sp. DH-1 grown in NMS medium with LA concentrations increasing from 0.5 to 8 g/L over 35 subcultures. pH adjusted to 6.8 with NaOH.
process_signals: Starting LA concentration: 0.5 g/L
Final LA concentration: 8 g/L
Number of subcultures: 35
pH maintained at 6.8
methods: Serial transfer in NMS medium with stepwise increasing LA.

section_gists.heading: Analytical methods
section_gists.gist: Cell growth measured by OD600. Metabolites analyzed by HPLC. Methane consumption measured by GC with TCD.

section_gists.heading: Availability of data and materials
section_gists.gist: All data included in article and additional files.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: LA toxicity is the primary bottleneck for high-level LA production in methanotrophs.
ALE can generate LA-tolerant strains by selecting for mutations that confer tolerance.
The 2-bp deletion in the watR promoter upregulates watR and downstream genes, leading to LA tolerance.
Overexpression of watR alone is sufficient to increase LA tolerance in wild-type.
Combining LA tolerance with heterologous D-LDH expression and glgA deletion enables significantly higher D-LA production.
Nutrient limitations (e.g., nitrogen) restrict growth and production in fed-batch, and addressing them can further improve titers.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What are the target genes of WatR that mediate LA tolerance?
How does the TT deletion enhance transcription of the watR operon?
Does the fliE nonsense mutation contribute to LA tolerance in JHM80?
What is the maximum achievable D-LA titer and productivity with further process optimization?
Can the watR overexpression strategy be applied to other methanotrophs?

events.summary: LA toxicity limits high-level LA production in methanotrophs.
Wild-type Methylomonas sp. DH-1 growth severely inhibited by LA, tolerance up to 0.5 g/L at pH 6.8.
Adaptive laboratory evolution performed by serial transfer with increasing LA from 0.5 to 8.0 g/L over 35 subcultures.
Evolved strains JHM30 and JHM80 isolated, tolerant to 3.0 and 8.0 g/L LA respectively.
Overexpression of watR in wild-type increased LA tolerance, confirming its role.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers problem statement, ALE experiment, genome analysis, and validation of watR role.

events.summary: Deletion of watR operon genes in JHM80 to test their role in LA tolerance.
Overexpression of watR, smtM, rstM in wild-type to verify role in LA tolerance.
Testing effect of fliE nonsense mutation on LA tolerance by deleting fliE in JHM30.
Integration of heterologous D-LDH genes into JHM80 chromosome with glgA deletion for D-LA production.
Comparison of D-LA production by JHM80 strains expressing different LDH genes.
Periodic methane feeding in JHM86 (JHM80 + Lm.LDH) increased D-LA titer to 750 mg/L.
Flask-scale production with pH control and extra nitrogen improved D-LA titer to 1.19 g/L.
Growth stopped after 96 h possibly due to nutrient depletion; extra nitrogen extended growth.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers key experiments: gene deletion/overexpression to validate watR role, LDH screening, production comparison, and process optimization with limitations.

events.summary: Comparison of D-LA production in JHM86 with previous studies shows highest titer and yield reported for LA from methane.
Intracellular LA production still toxic in JHM80 despite external tolerance up to 8 g/L, suggesting different tolerance mechanisms.
Hypothesis that lactate production inhibits growth via intracellular acidification and metabolic effects, and redirection of pyruvate flux reduces TCA cycle.
Further elucidation of LA tolerance mechanisms and metabolic pathways needed to improve LA production.
Proposed strategies for further enhancement: regulation of LDH expression, elimination of competing pathways, and fermentation optimization.
Overexpression of watR identified as responsible for increased LA tolerance in JHM80.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains discussion, conclusions, and methods sections. Extracted events focus on analysis of results, limitations, hypotheses, and design proposals.
This chunk contains only references, publisher's note, and author affiliations. No research evolution events are present.

evidence_bank.quote: LA toxicity is one of the limiting factors for high-level production of LA.
The cell growth was severely inhibited by addition of LA, exhibiting low tolerance up to 0.5 g/L LA in the medium neutralized to pH 6.8 (Fig. 1b).
we performed adaptive laboratory evolution of Methylomonas sp. DH-1 by serially transferring cells to the medium with increasing concentrations of LA from 0.5 g/L to 8.0 g/L.
evolved strains JHM30 and JHM80 were selected, which could survive at 3.0 g/L and 8.0 g/L LA, respectively, during the evolution process.
Overexpression of the watR gene in wild-type strain also led to an increase in LA tolerance.
deletion of all three genes abolished the LA tolerance of JHM80, suggesting that the elevated expression of watR is mainly responsible for the LA tolerance of JHM80
cells integrated with the OE1 and OE3 expression cassettes showed higher LA tolerance
overexpression of OE2 failed to recover the LA sensitivity of the wild-type strain
fliE deletion did not improve the LA tolerance of JHM30

4 heterologous D form-specific LDH genes from L. jensenii (Lj1. LDH and Lj3.LDH), L. delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus ATCC 11842 (Ld.LDH), L. mesenteroides subsp. mesenteroides ATCC 8293 (Lm.LDH) were introduced into the chromosome of JHM80 while deleting the glgA gene
JHM80 strains expressing Lm.LDH and Ld.LDH were more effective in LA production than the strains expressing the other LDH genes, producing 192 mg/L and 187 mg/L LA, respectively
JHM86 strain produced 750 mg/L D-LA after 118 h
wild-type strain integrated with Lm.LDH (JHM14) showed very low growth rate and produced only 100 mg/L D-LA
JHM86 continued growth up to 120 h, producing 1.19 g/L LA at 144 h with a productivity of 0.008 g/L h
JHM86 strain stopped growth after 96 h, which could be due to the depletion of other nutrients such as nitrogen source
our study showed the highest titer and yield ever reported in LA production from methane.

JHM80 could survive in the presence of 8 g/L LA at pH 6.8, but the JHM80-derived LA-production strain JHM86 showed reduced growth rate although its LA production level was below the maximum tolerance level
suggesting that intracellular production of LA is still toxic in JHM80 strain.
Lactate production might inhibit cell growth through intracellular acidification and various metabolic effects of acid anion as suggested in other microorganisms
redirection of the pyruvate flux to lactate might reduce the downstream metabolic pathways including the TCA cycle, thus affecting cell growth.
further elucidation of LA tolerance mechanisms and metabolic pathways might be necessary to improve LA production.

LA production could be further enhanced by metabolic pathway engineering including the regulation of LDH gene expression levels, elimination of competing pathways, and optimization of fermentation medium and conditions.
overexpression of the watR gene encoding a LysR-type transcription factor was identified to be responsible for the increased LA tolerance of JHM80.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Lactate toxicity limits high-level lactate production in methanotrophs, and prior titers from methane were <1 g/L.

canonical_events.summary: Lactate toxicity limits high-level lactate production in methanotrophs; prior titers <1 g/L.
Wild-type Methylomonas sp. DH-1 growth severely inhibited by lactate; tolerance only up to 0.5 g/L at pH 6.8.
Adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) by serial transfer with increasing lactate (0.5 to 8.0 g/L) over 35 subcultures.
Isolation of evolved strains JHM30 (tolerant to 3.0 g/L) and JHM80 (tolerant to 8.0 g/L).
Whole genome sequencing reveals a 2-bp (TT) deletion in the intergenic region upstream of watR operon in JHM30 and JHM80; JHM80 also has a nonsense mutation in fliE.
Deletion of watR operon genes in JHM80 abolishes lactate tolerance, confirming watR upregulation is mainly responsible.
Overexpression of watR alone in wild-type increases lactate tolerance, confirming its role.
Deletion of fliE in JHM30 does not improve lactate tolerance, indicating fliE mutation is not responsible.
Integration of heterologous D-LDH genes (Lm.LDH, Ld.LDH, Lj1.LDH, Lj3.LDH) into JHM80 chromosome with glgA deletion for D-lactate production.
Comparison of D-lactate production: JHM80 strains expressing Lm.LDH and Ld.LDH produce 192 and 187 mg/L, respectively.
JHM86 (JHM80 + Lm.LDH + ΔglgA) produces 750 mg/L D-lactate with periodic methane feeding, 7.5-fold higher than wild-type equivalent (JHM14, 100 mg/L).
Flask-scale optimization with pH control (NaOH) and extra nitrogen (2× KNO3) improves D-lactate titer to 1.19 g/L, yield 0.245 g/g CH4, productivity 0.008 g/L h.
Growth stops after 96 h likely due to nutrient depletion (nitrogen); extra nitrogen extends growth to 120 h but productivity remains low (0.01 g/L h).
Intracellular lactate production still toxic in JHM80 despite external tolerance up to 8 g/L; watR target genes and tolerance mechanism remain unknown.

edges.reason: Problem of lactate toxicity motivates testing wild-type tolerance.
Low tolerance prompts adaptive laboratory evolution.
ALE yields tolerant strains JHM30 and JHM80.
Evolved strains sequenced to identify mutations.
TT deletion in watR promoter leads to testing its role via deletion.
Deletion confirms watR role; overexpression further validates.
fliE mutation also found; tested but not responsible.
Tolerant strain JHM80 used as host for metabolic engineering.
LDH genes integrated and compared for production.
Best LDH (Lm.LDH) used in JHM86 for fed-batch production.
Process optimization improves titer and yield.
Despite external tolerance, intracellular toxicity persists.

must_cover_points: Lactate toxicity limits production in methanotrophs.
ALE generates tolerant strains JHM30 and JHM80.
2-bp deletion in watR promoter identified and validated as key tolerance mechanism.
Metabolic engineering with D-LDH and glgA deletion in JHM80 enables D-lactate production.
JHM86 achieves 7.5-fold higher titer than wild-type equivalent.
Process optimization yields 1.19 g/L, 0.245 g/g CH4.
Limitations: nutrient depletion, intracellular toxicity, unknown watR targets.
